concepts.title: Equation of Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of Wave Motion 

Some physical quantity (say y) is made to oscillate at one place and these oscillations of y propagate to other places. The y may be,

(i) displacement of particles from their mean position in case of transverse wave in a rope or longitudinal sound wave in a gas.

(ii) pressure difference $\left( \text{dP} \right)$ or density difference $\left( \text{d}\rho \right)$ in case of sound wave or

(iii) electric and magnetic fields in case of electromagnetic waves. The oscillations of y may or may not be simple harmonic in nature. Now let us consider a one dimensional wave travelling along $\text{x}$-axis. In this case $\text{y}$ is a function of position $\left( \text{x} \right)$ and time $\left( \text{t} \right)$. The reason is that one may be interested in knowing the value of $\text{y}$ at a general point $\text{x}$ at any time t. Thus, we can say that,

$\text{y}=\text{y}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)$

But only those functions of $\text{x}$ and $\text{t}$, represent a wave motion which satisfy the differential equation.

$\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}y}{\partial {{t}^{2}}}=k\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}y}{\partial {{x}^{2}}}$

Here $\text{k}$ is a constant, which is equal to square of the wave speed, or

$\text{k}={{\text{v}}^{2}}$

Thus, the above equation can be written as,

$\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}y}{\partial {{t}^{2}}}={{v}^{2}}\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}y}{\partial {{x}^{2}}}$ … (i)

The general solution of this equation is of the form $y\left( x,t \right)=f\left( ax\pm bt \right)$ ... (ii)

Thus, any function of $\text{x}$ and $\text{t}$ which satisfies Eq. (i) or which can be written as Eq. (ii) represents a wave. The only condition is that it should be finite everywhere and at all times. Further, if these conditions are satisfied, then speed of wave $\left( v \right)$ is given by,

$v=\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }t}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }x}=\frac{b}{a}$

The plus (+) sign between ax and bt implies that the wave is travelling along - ve x-direction and minus (-) sign shows that it is travelling along +ve x-direction.

concepts.title: Plane Progressive Harmonic Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Plane Progressive Harmonic Wave 

If a travelling wave is a sin or cos function of ( $at-bx)$ or (at $+bx)$, the wave is said to be harmonic or plane progressive wave. Here we shall limit ourselves to $1-\text{D}$ plane progressive wave which in its most general form is given by

$\text{y}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{tkx}\mp \phi \right)$

From Eqn. it is clear that a set of four parameters A, $\phi ,\omega $ and $\text{k}$ completely describes a plane progressive wave.

(1) As the maximum value of sin or cos functions can be 1, A represents the maximum value of wave-function as is called the amplitude of the wave.

(2) The constant $\phi $ is called phase constant or initial phase and enables us to find the position from where time is considered. If at $t=0,x=0,\phi $ will be zero which is usually the case with a wave and implies that in wave motion time is considered when the wave was at the origin. Henceforth we shall assume $\phi =0$ and the wave is travelling along positive $\text{x}$-axis unless stated otherwise.

(3) As the wave at a given position at time t' [Fig. A] will be

$\text{{y}'}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{{t}'}-\text{kx} \right)$

So the wave will repeat itself if $\text{{y}'}=\text{y}$, i.e., $\text{{t}'}=\text{t}+\left( 2\pi /\omega \right)$ as $\text{sin}\left( \theta +2\pi \right)=\text{sin}\theta $.

Now as the time after which a wave repeats itself is called time period, i.e.

$\text{T}=\text{{t}'}-\text{t}=\left( 2\pi /\omega \right)$

Further as the rate at which the wave repeats itself is called its frequency $\text{f}$ (with units $\text{Hz}$ ) so

$\text{f}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}=\frac{\omega }{2\pi }$

or $\omega =2\pi f,\omega $ is called angular frequency (with units $\text{rad}/\text{s}$ ). Here it is worth noting that $\omega ,\text{f}$ or $\text{T}$ are the characteristics of the source producing the wave and are independent of the nature of the medium in which the wave propagates.

(4) As the wave at a given time at position ${x}'$ [Fig.(B)] will be

$\text{{y}'}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{k}{{\text{x}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}} \right)$

So the wave will repeat itself if $y={y}'$, i.e., ${x}'=x+\left( 2\pi /k \right)$ as $\text{sin}\left( \theta \pm 2\pi \right)=\text{sin}\theta $. Now as the distance after which the wave repeats itself is called wavelength $\lambda $, 

So $\lambda ={x}'-x=\frac{2\pi }{k},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ k}=\frac{2\pi }{\lambda }$ … (5)

$\text{k}$ is called propagation constant or wave vector and has unit ( $rad/m)$. The constant $\text{k}$ or wavelength $\lambda $ depends on the nature of the medium (as same source will produce waves of different wavelengths in different media) and also on the source producing the waves (as in a given medium sources of different frequencies will produce different wavelengths).

(5) If the shape of the wave does not change as the wave propagates in a medium, with increase in $t,x$ will also increase in such a way that

$\omega t-\text{kx}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ constt}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

The argument of harmonic function $(\omega t-\text{kx}$ ) is called phase of the wave and is constant if the shape of the wave remains unchanged. Further, if we consider two points at positions ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{x}}_{2}}$ on a wave at a given instant, then

${{\phi }_{1}}=\omega \text{t}-\text{k}{{\text{x}}_{1}}$ and ${{\phi }_{2}}=\omega \text{t}-\text{k}{{\text{x}}_{2}}$

So ${{\phi }_{2}}\sim {{\phi }_{1}}=\text{k}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\phi =\frac{2\pi }{\lambda }\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ k}=\frac{2\pi }{\lambda } \right]$ … (7)

From this it is clear that if $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}=\lambda ,\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\phi =2\pi $, i.e., a path difference $\lambda $ corresponds to a phase change

(6) As the phase of a plane progressive wave is constt., i.e.,

$\omega \text{t}-\text{kx}=\text{const}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

the so called wave or phase velocity will be given by

$\text{v}=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\frac{\omega }{\text{k}}=\frac{2\pi \text{f}}{\left( 2\pi /\lambda \right)}=\text{f}\lambda $ … (8)

(7) As a plane progressive wave propagating along positive $\text{x}$-axis with $\text{t}=0$ at $\text{x}=0$ is given by

$\text{y}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)$

so the velocity of a particle on it will be ${{\text{v}}_{\text{Pa}}}=\frac{dy}{dt}=\text{A}\omega \text{cos}\left( \omega t-\text{kx} \right)$ … (10)

But as $\text{cos}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)=\sqrt{\left\{ 1-\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right) \right\}}=\sqrt{1-{{(\text{y}/\text{A})}^{2}}}$

So ${{\text{v}}_{\text{Pa}}}=\omega \sqrt{\left( {{\text{A}}^{2}}-{{\text{y}}^{2}} \right)}$

It will be maximum when ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=\text{min}=0$

i.e., ${{\left( {{\text{v}}_{\text{Pa}}} \right)}_{\text{max}}}=\text{A}\omega $

The acceleration of the particle is the second partial derivative of $\text{y}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)$ with respect to $\text{t}$,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{a}_{P}}=\frac{{{\partial }^{2}}y\left( x,t \right)}{\partial {{t}^{2}}}$$~=-{{\omega }^{2}}A\text{sin}\left( kx-wt \right)$

$~=-{{\omega }^{2}}y$

i.e. the acceleration of the particle equals $-{{\omega }^{2}}$ times its displacement. Thus

${{\text{a}}_{\text{P}}}=-{{\omega }^{2}}\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ displacement }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

(8) A plane progressive wave (either transverse of longitudinal, mechanical, non-mechanical) in the light of above can be written in many forms such as

$\text{y}=\text{Asin}\left[ \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right]$

Or $\text{y}=\text{Asin}2\pi \left[ \text{ft}-\text{x}/\lambda \right)]$ [as $\omega =2\pi \text{f}$ and $\text{k}=\left( 2\pi /\lambda \right)]$ 
or $\text{y}=\text{Asin}2\pi \left[ \frac{\text{t}}{\text{T}}-\frac{\text{x}}{\lambda } \right]$ [as $\text{f}=1/\text{T}]$ 
or $\text{y}=\text{Asink}\left[ \text{vt}-\text{x} \right]$ [as in (a), $\text{v}=\omega /\text{k}]$ 
or $\text{y}=\text{Asin}\omega \left[ \text{t}-\left( \text{x}/\text{v} \right) \right]$ [as in $\left( \text{a} \right),\text{k}/\omega =1/\text{v}]$

Note: In solving numerical problems related to equation of plane progressive wave remember that:

(1) If the sign between $t$ and $x$ terms is negative the wave is propagating along positive $x$-axis and vice-versa.

(2) The coefficient of $\text{sin}$ or cos function, i.e., A gives the amplitude of the wave while its argument $\left( \omega \text{t}\mp \text{kx} \right)$ denotes phase.

(3) The coefficient of $t$ gives angular frequency $\omega \left[ =2\pi f=\left( 2\pi /T \right) \right]$.

(4) The coefficient of $x$ gives propagation constant or wave number $k\left( =2\pi /\lambda \right)$.

(5) The ratio of coefficient of to that of $x$ gives wave or phase velocity, i.e., $v=\left( \omega /\text{k} \right)$ and is constant for a given medium.

(6) Particle velocity will be obtained by differentiating y with respect to t, i.e., ${{\text{v}}_{\text{Pa}}}=\left( \text{dy}/\text{dt} \right)=$ $-\text{v}$ (slope of $\text{y}/\text{x}$ curve) and is not constt. with maximum value $=\text{A}\omega $.

(7) When a given wave passes from one medium to another its frequency does not change, so from $\text{v}=\text{f}\lambda $

$\left( {{\text{v}}_{1}} \right)/\left( {{\text{v}}_{2}} \right)=\left( {{\lambda }_{1}} \right)/\left( {{\lambda }_{2}} \right)$

(8) In case of vibrations of string (in its fundamental mode)

$\lambda =2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\![\!\!\text{ length }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ string }\!\!]\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

(9) While passing equation $\text{v}=\text{f}\lambda $, be careful to decide which out of $\text{v},\text{f}$ and $\lambda $ is constant

(10) Figure shows the velocity $\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{P}}} \right)$ and acceleration $\left( {{\text{a}}_{\text{P}}} \right)$ for two points 1 and 2 on a string as a sinusoidal wave is travelling in it along positive $\text{x}$-direction.

At 1: Slope of the curve is positive. Hence particle velocity $\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{P}}} \right)$ is negative or downwards. Similarly, displacement of the particle is positive, so acceleration will be negative or downwards.

At 2: Slope is negative while displacement is positive. Hence ${{\text{V}}_{\text{F}}}$ will be positive (upwards) and ${{\text{a}}_{\text{P}}}$ is negative (downwards).

Note : The direction of ${{\text{V}}_{\text{P}}}$ will change if the wave travels along negative $\text{x}$-direction.

concepts.title: Energy of Plane Progressive Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Energy of a Plane Progressive Wave 

Consider a plane wave propagating with velocity $\text{v}$ in $\text{x}$-direction across an area s. An element of material medium (density $\left. =\rho \text{kg}/{{\text{m}}^{3}} \right)$ will have a mass $\rho \left( \text{sdx} \right)$

The displacement of a particle from its equilibrium position is given by the wave equation.

$\text{y}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)$

 

Total energy, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dE}=\frac{1}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}_{\text{max}}^{2}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{dE} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\left( \rho \text{sdx} \right){{(\text{A}\omega )}^{2}} \\ {} & ~=\rho \text{sdx}\left( 2{{\pi }^{2}}{{\text{f}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}^{2}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ energy density $=\frac{dE}{sdx}=2{{\pi }^{2}}{{f}^{2}}{{A}^{2}}\rho \left( J/{{m}^{3}} \right)$

energy per unit length $=\rho s\left( 2{{\pi }^{2}}{{\text{f}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}^{2}} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ power transmitted $=2{{\pi }^{2}}{{\text{f}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}^{2}}\rho \text{sv}\left( \text{J}/\text{s} \right)$

concepts.title: Intensity of Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Intensity of the Wave (I) 

Intensity of the wave is defined as the power crossing per unit area.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=2{{\pi }^{2}}{{\text{f}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}^{2}}\rho \text{v}\left( \text{Watt}/{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)$

For wave propagation through taut string

$\rho s=\mu $, the linear density in $\text{kg}/\text{m}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Energy per unit length $=2{{\pi }^{2}}{{\text{f}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{A}}^{2}}\mu $

Note: 

(1) The energy is the average value over a time period

(2) Intensity I $\propto {{\text{A}}^{2}}$

( $\mu $ and $f$ are constant)
